caption.text: You just launched a new feature,
it took you several weeks of dev,
the whole team is proud. Except that a week later,
no one is using it. And at that point the reflex is to say:
we’ll send an email,
we’ll make an announcement or we’ll add a pop-up.
But that’s not really the problem.
The problem is you put a feature into your
product without thinking about how your user would discover it,
understand it and above all realize what it changes for them.
A feature that isn’t adopted is a
invisible feature. And for it to be adopted,
there are 4 things to do.
First, you have to put it on your user’s path.
Not in a side menu or in a
notification they’ll ignore,
but on the path they already take every day.
For example, in a SaaS for HR,
if you launch a candidate scoring feature,
it has to appear directly in the candidate list,
where your user goes every morning.
Second, you have to tell them what it changes for them, not what it does.
So you don’t say
“discover automatic scoring”,
but “find your best candidates in 30 seconds”.
The difference is between describing a feature and selling a result.
Third, you have to give them a reason to try it now.
So not “learn more” or “watch the demo”, but an action
that’s immediate, in the context they’re in.
“Score your current candidates in 1 click”. And fourth,
you create a dedicated mini onboarding for it.
Once they’ve clicked,
you don’t leave them to figure it out on their own.
2 or 3 steps max,
just enough for them to reach the value of the feature
before they’ve fully set everything up.
Because launching a feature
is not just development, it’s also journey design.
